old_claim.note: Pneumatic Tools provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct pneumatic_tools_compressed_air_power dependency remains because the source-backed node is scoped to Tweddell-era hydraulic riveting for industrial boilers, bridges, and shipyards, not later pneumatic rivet-gun practice.

source_shape.source_locator: The reproduced obituaries describe Tweddell's 1865 stationary hydraulic riveting machine, 1871 portable riveter, pumps, accumulators, and hydraulic pressure applications to boilers, bridges, and ships.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds the corrected industrial riveting node in hydraulic riveting machines rather than compressed-air pneumatic tools.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pneumatic tools may support later rivet-gun practice, but the corrected node's chronology and mechanism are hydraulic, so compressed-air tools are not a direct prerequisite.

ontology_before: Riveting Technology depended directly on Pneumatic Tools, implying compressed-air tools enabled the industrial riveting node.
ontology_after: Riveting Technology is scoped to hydraulic/mechanized industrial riveting and depends on metal platework, steel production, and steam-boiler pressure context without a pneumatic-tools prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the direct pneumatic_tools_compressed_air_power dependency is removed from riveting_technology.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to twentieth-century pneumatic rivet guns rather than Tweddell-era hydraulic riveting.
A source shows compressed-air pneumatic riveters enabled the 1860s hydraulic riveting system.
The graph adds a narrower pneumatic-rivet-gun node that should replace the broad riveting_technology node.
short_reason: The source-backed industrial riveting system used hydraulic pressure, not compressed-air pneumatic tools.
